1: Micah prophesied ca. 735-700 BCE, contemporary with Isaiah in the southern kingdom, during the Assyrian crisis that destroyed Samaria (722 BCE) and threatened Jerusalem (701 BCE). He was a rural prophet from Moresheth-Gath (a village in the Judean foothills), which gives his oracles a social-justice perspective: he speaks for the peasant farmers whose land is being seized by the powerful (2:1-2; 3:1-3). His prophecy of Jerusalem's destruction (3:12: Zion shall be plowed as a field; Jerusalem shall become a heap of ruins) was so striking that it was cited a century later as a precedent for not killing Jeremiah when he prophesied similarly (Jer 26:18-19). Micah 6:8 is the OT's most compact ethical summary and one of the most frequently cited prophetic verses.
2: 'Hear, all you peoples; pay attention, O earth, and all that is in it' — the summons to a cosmic court. Micah opens with a call that encompasses the entire earth as witness, not just Israel and Judah. YHWH comes forth from his place (his heavenly temple) and will tread upon the high places of the earth — a theophanic image closely paralleling Amos 4:13 ('he who treads on the heights of the earth') and Deut 33:29. The calling of the earth as witness to YHWH's case against his own people follows the pattern of Deut 32:1 (Moses' Song) and Isa 1:2.
3: 'For behold, YHWH is coming out of his place and will come down and tread upon the high places of the earth.' The language of divine descent for judgment is common in theophany texts (Gen 11:5, 18:21; Exod 3:8; Ps 144:5). The 'high places' (bamot) are both literal mountain summits and the cultic high places where illicit worship occurred — YHWH's theophanic tread will shatter both geography and false cult simultaneously.
4: The mountains melt like wax before fire; the valleys split open. This volcanic and seismic imagery is theophanic (cf. Ps 97:5; Nah 1:5; Judg 5:5 — the Sinai tradition of YHWH causing mountains to melt). The imagery deliberately recalls Sinai-style divine presence: YHWH is approaching his people with the same power that established the covenant — but now to execute its curses rather than establish its promises.
5: 'All this is for the transgression of Jacob and for the sins of the house of Israel.' The twofold designation — transgression of Jacob / sin of house of Israel — encompasses the whole covenant people. But immediately Micah narrows the blame to leadership centers: 'What is the transgression of Jacob? Is it not Samaria? And what is the high place of Judah? Is it not Jerusalem?' Samaria (northern capital) and Jerusalem (southern capital) are named as the sources of corruption that contaminate the whole land — a prophet from the rural fringe indicting both urban centers.
6: Samaria's punishment is announced: she will become a heap of ruins in the open country and a planting of vineyards. Samaria was indeed destroyed by Sargon II of Assyria in 722 BCE (2 Kgs 17:5-6). The archaeological record confirms Samaria's destruction and depopulation. Micah's prophecy here is the most direct announcement of the northern capital's fate in the pre-722 prophetic corpus. 'Her stones into the valley' and 'her foundations laid bare' describe systematic demolition.
7: Samaria's 'gathered wages' are destroyed — Micah describes Samaria's wealth as the wages of a prostitute (etnan zonah), money given for cultic prostitution that fed the temple treasury. The money gathered in harlotry will return to hire new prostitutes: the corrupt wealth cycle continues even in destruction. 'All her images will be beaten to pieces, all her idols burned with fire': the language of Deuteronomic reform (Deut 7:5, 25). This will be 'gathered from the wages of a prostitute, and to the wages of a prostitute they shall return' — a grim economic irony.
8: Micah's personal lamentation shifts the oracle from third person announcement to first person mourning: 'For this I will lament and wail; I will go stripped and naked; I will make lamentation like the jackals, and mourning like the ostriches.' The prophet's stripping himself and wailing like a jackal or ostrich (known for eerie nocturnal cries) was a recognized form of public prophetic mourning that dramatized the oracle. This performance of grief authenticates the prophet's sorrow as genuine rather than detached.
9: 'For her wound is incurable, and it has come to Judah; it has reached to the gate of my people, to Jerusalem.' Samaria's corruption is not contained — it has spread south to Judah and reached Jerusalem's very gates. The 'wound' (makkah) is used of plague or military defeat; its incurable character (anushit) echoes Jer 15:18 (Jeremiah's own 'incurable wound'). Micah introduces the contagion model: Samaria's sin is infectious, spreading south.
10: Micah's famous wordplay lament: 'Tell it not in Gath' — echoing David's lament over Saul (2 Sam 1:20 'tell it not in Gath'). The passage continues with a series of town-name puns in the Shephelah region — Micah's home territory. Gath / tagidu (tell), Beth-le-aphrah / aphar (dust, 'in Beth of Dust, roll in the dust'), etc. These are the towns of Micah's homeland, and each receives a wordplay oracle about its own judgment. The lament is hyper-local: Micah is not prophesying abstractly but about the specific towns he grew up near.
11: Shaphir — wordplay on shaphar (beautiful): 'Pass on your way, inhabitant of Shaphir [Beautiful], in nakedness and shame.' Zaanan — 'does not come out' (wordplay on yatza). Beth-ezel — 'its standing place is taken away' (wordplay on etzel). The sequence of villages and their wordplays creates a rapid-fire lamentation that reads like a litany of local grief. Each village name becomes a caption for its own disaster.
12: Maroth — 'the inhabitant of Maroth writhes in pain' (wordplay on marah, bitter, or mar, pain). 'For disaster has come down from YHWH to the gate of Jerusalem.' The oracle swings back from the Shephelah towns to the ultimate target: the disaster sweeping through Micah's home region is en route to Jerusalem. The series of town puns serves as a geographic trail marking the Assyrian advance south toward the capital.
13: Lachish — 'harness the steeds to the chariots, inhabitant of Lachish.' Lachish was the most significant fortified city in the Judean Shephelah and a major Assyrian military target. Sennacherib's assault on Lachish (701 BCE) was commemorated in famous palace reliefs (now in the British Museum). Micah accuses Lachish of being the 'beginning of sin to the daughter of Zion' — implying that Lachish was where the cavalry-and-chariot military culture that replaced trust in YHWH entered Judah (cf. Deut 17:16; Isa 31:1-3).
14: Moresheth-Gath — Micah's own hometown — receives a farewell oracle: 'Therefore you shall give parting gifts to Moresheth-Gath.' The Hebrew shiluchim (parting gifts / divorce settlement) is exactly the dowry paid when a bride leaves her father's house, or the payment given when a wife is divorced. Moresheth-Gath will be given up — Israel is divorcing its own land. Achzib — 'a lie to the kings of Israel' (wordplay on achziv, lie/disappointment) — a town name punning on its destiny as false hope.
15: Mareshah — 'I will again bring a conqueror to you' (wordplay on yoresh, conqueror/heir). 'The glory of Israel shall come to Adullam' — Adullam was the cave where David fled from Saul (1 Sam 22:1), a place of refuge and royal exile. Micah ironically inverts this: Israel's glory (its nobles, its leadership) will flee there as refugees, as David once did. The phrase 'glory of Israel' (kevod Yisrael) is deliberately degraded by its destination — a cave of exile.
16: 'Make yourselves bald and cut off your hair, for the children of your delight; make yourselves as bald as the eagle, for they shall go from you into exile.' Shaving the head was a standard mourning practice in the ancient Near East (cf. Amos 8:10; Isa 22:12; Job 1:20). The 'eagle' (nesher) — likely the griffon vulture, whose bald head was a recognizable sight — provides the vivid comparison. Ch. 1 ends with the image of Judah's mothers mourning children deported into Assyrian exile.
